note:
回顾1971年Ram Dass的经典著作《 Be Here Now》，以及该书与信息和我们的生存状态的联系。
  就像许多走过精英机构走廊的人一样，拉姆·达斯（Ram Dass）的生活与本世纪中叶有影响力的思想家和行动者相交。他最著名的合作者可能是1960年代初的蒂莫西·里里（Timothy Leary）。他们对迷幻药物的里程碑式研究导致他于1963年被哈佛开除。
 Ram Dass与David Padwa的联系对我更直接感兴趣。 Padwa成立了一家名为Basic Systems的公司，并在1960年代末与这位年轻作家见面时成为百万富翁。摘自David Padwa的履历表：“ 1960-1965。 Basic Systems Inc.的创始人兼首席执行官。主要办公室位于剑桥，纽约，芝加哥，帕萨迪纳和华盛顿特区。该公司专门从事教育和课程技术，以及在教育和培训市场中提供系统集成服务。 1964年，施乐公司收购了基本系统公司；继续担任Xerox部门主管和教育市场计划总监。”基本系统（Basic Systems）使员工培训和学生教育自动化，并且通常集成到机构的现有计算机基础结构中。
 该公司引起了施乐公司的注意，并很快被收购。它非常适合施乐不断增长的数字产品组合。当复印机公司寻求建立未来的办公室时，它将很快收购有影响力的小型计算机制造商Scientific Data Systems，并建立传奇的Xerox PARC。
  Padwa与有影响力的心理学家Francis Mechner共同创立了Basic Systems。我小时候第一次在我的Atari 7800上玩Karateka（1984）时接触过Francis Mechner。原始版本由Jordan Mechner编写，并由Broderbund于1984年出版。Atari 7800版本由Ibid Inc.的Jack Jackberg，Sr.编写。并由Atari于1987年出版。后来又由我的个人计算机在波斯王子（1989年）中出版。弗朗西斯·梅希纳（Francis Mechner）是这些游戏的作曲家，均由他的儿子乔丹·梅希纳（Jordan Mechner）撰写。迈赫纳（Mechner）的长者有点像个文艺复兴时期的人：开办了几家成功的公司，会说五种语言，发表跨学科的理论见解，担任钢琴演奏家，并在Go中获得5得分。 Padwa和Mechner的公司还于1966年出版了“ Bobby Fischer教授国际象棋”的原始版。畅销书至今仍在印刷中。
 这些角色提供了培育Ram Dass的环境的样本。他将很快离开这些舒适环境，与Padwa和他的Land Rover一起从德黑兰到印度的公路旅行中，寻求精神上的唤醒。
 拉姆·达斯（Ram Dass）最终在印度呆了一段时间（就像帕德瓦（Padwa）的路虎（Land Rover）一样），这导致了《 Be Here Now》一书的诞生。这本书的影响力毫无疑问。它是美国反文化运动的重要贡献者，也是许多西方佛教和瑜伽练习者的起点。 
拉姆·达斯（Ram Dass）花费了本书的第一部分来描述他的旅程。它涉及到迷幻药物及其益处和局限性的世界。他的经历本质上使他对东方神秘主义敞开了怀抱。在通过中亚和印度的道路上，他发现出奇的奇特小兵出奇地少。这本书的关键专家通过“不教”的过程拓宽了拉姆·达斯的思想，因为“他给我的教训是肯定的，好像我已经知道了一切。”
 作者发现老师和老师之间的重要区别。 “老师指路”，而“老师就是路”。拉姆·达斯（Ram Dass）的觉醒，以及这本书，都是大师们关心的结果。
 这导致Ram Dass试图唤醒读者的是精美插图但公开教条式的部分。作者的意图源于佛教长期以来的实践，即改变实践者对世界的习惯性和毫无疑问的认识。上师为此提供了一些巧妙的措辞，“如果扒手遇见圣人，他只会看见他的口袋”和“如果您穿鞋皮，那么整个地球就会被皮革覆盖。”拉姆·达斯（Ram Dass）的精神青年在这里最为明显。他没有老师或老师的语言；一个充满热情的讲师的声音深信他已经发现了真相。
 本书的最后一部分是引文和技巧的集合，这些引言和技巧被分为各种“神圣生命的成分”，例如睡眠，真理，时空，普拉纳亚姆等。这是令人愉快的阅读。
 拉姆·达斯（Ram Dass）清楚地表明，如果一个人真的想知道《现在就在这里》所包含的智慧，就不会有谁知道。将主体与客体分开的理性思维是一种错觉。 （Loc。4594，4613）这种错觉是由对自我的一种特殊和完全独立于一切的依恋所驱动的。
   Atari 7800的Karateka墨盒内的深处是驱动机器的说明列表。但是程序员确实将一条人类可读的消息与计算机代码混合在一起：“妈妈和我合而为一”。这种奇怪的说法是Atari 7800墨盒所独有的。它没有出现在其他版本中。它永远不会出现在屏幕上。它仅对正在查看其中包含的代码的用户可见。 “ Digital Press Easter Eggs：Karateka”，digitpress.com，2010年。
 这句话几乎可以肯定与劳埃德·西尔弗曼（Lloyd Silverman）和乔尔·温伯格（Joel Weinberger）在1985年的美国心理学家杂志上发表的一篇论文有关，该论文的标题为“妈妈和我是一个人：对心理治疗的影响”。作者断言，标题式信息即使在潜移默化中也可以帮助激励人们。 
无数的研究试图证明“一体”的好处。 对于母亲而言，统一是不言而喻的。 我们都在一个点上处于物理联系。 DNA密码已经在我们内心深处存在了数千年，直接从母亲传给孩子。 这是为意识奠定基础的古老信息。  比我们还大的意识的现实是“现在就在这里”的根源。 但是，拉姆·达斯（Ram Dass）并未确立上帝或单一的方法论，而是不断宣称，自我使我们看不见了遍布我们日常经验的庞大联系网络。 揭开面纱的方法是简单地练习当下的生活。 或正如铃木（Ruzuki Roshi）所说，“一名禅宗学生必须学会认真地浪费时间。” （位置4728）  这篇文章中的事件已添加到信息历史上重要事件的时间轴中。
note_en:
A reflection on Ram Dass’ 1971 classic,  Be Here Now, and the book’s connection to information and our state of being.
  Like many who walk the hallways of elite institutions, Ram Dass’ life intersected with influential mid-century thinkers and doers. His most famous collaborator might have been Timothy Leary in the early 1960s. Their landmark studies of psychedelic drugs lead to his dismissal from Harvard in 1963.
 Ram Dass’ association with David Padwa is of more immediate interest to me. Padwa established a company called Basic Systems and became a millionaire by the time he met the young author in the late 1960s.    From David Padwa’s  Curriculum Vitae: “1960-1965. Founder and CEO of Basic Systems Inc. Principal offices in Cambridge, New York, Chicago, Pasadena, and Washington, DC. Firm specialized in educational and curricular technologies as well as the provision of systems integration services in education and training markets. In 1964 Xerox Corporation acquired Basic Systems; continued as division executive at Xerox and Director of Planning for educational markets.”   Basic Systems automated employee training and student education, often integrating into an institution’s existing computer infrastructure.
 The company caught the eye of Xerox Corporation and was soon acquired. It fit nicely with Xerox’s growing digital portfolio. As the copier company sought to establish the office of the future, it would soon acquire the influential minicomputer manufacturer  Scientific Data Systems and establish the legendary  Xerox PARC.
  Padwa founded Basic Systems with the influential psychologist Francis Mechner. I was first exposed to Francis Mechner as a child playing  Karateka (1984) on my Atari 7800    The original version was written by Jordan Mechner and published by Broderbund in 1984. The Atari 7800 version was written by Jack Sandberg, Sr. at Ibid Inc. and published by Atari in 1987.   and then later  Prince of Persia (1989) on my family’s PC. Francis Mechner was the composer for these games, both written by his son  Jordan Mechner. The elder Mechner is something of a renaissance man: starting several successful businesses, speaking five languages, publishing cross-disciplinary theoretical insights, performing as a concert pianist, and achieving a 5-dan rating in Go.    Padwa and Mechner’s company also published the  original edition of “Bobby Fischer Teaches Chess” in 1966. The best-selling book is still in print today.
 These characters provide a sampling of the environment that nurtured Ram Dass. He would soon leave these comforts to join Padwa and his Land Rover on a road trip from Tehran to India in search of a spiritual awakening.
 Ram Dass ultimately stayed in India for some time (as did Padwa’s Land Rover), leading to the insights that birthed the book  Be Here Now. This book’s influence is without question. It was a significant contributor to the American counterculture movement and a starting point for many Western practitioners of Buddhism and Yoga.
   Ram Dass spends the first part of the book describing his journey. It involves a dive into the world of psychedelic drugs and their benefits and limitations. His experience essentially opens him up to Eastern mysticism. He finds surprisingly few charlatans on the road through Central Asia and India. The book’s pivotal guru broadens Ram Dass’ mind through a process of “no teaching” because “his lessons aroused in me just affirmation… as if I knew it all already.”
 The author strikes an important difference between  teacher and  guru. “A teacher points the way” while “a guru is the way.” Ram Dass’ awakening, and this book, is the result of the guru’s care.
 This leads to a beautifully illustrated but overtly dogmatic section where Ram Dass attempts wake the reader up. The writer’s intention is rooted in Buddhism’s long practice of shifting the practitioner’s habitual and unquestioned perception of the world.    The guru offers a few clever turns of phrases in this effort, “If a pickpocket meets a saint, he sees only his pockets” and “If you wear shoe leather, the whole earth is covered with leather.”   Ram Dass’ spiritual youth feels most pronounced here. He doesn’t have the language of a guru or a teacher; it is the voice of an enthusiastic lecturer who is convinced that he has uncovered the Truth.
 The final section of the book is a collection of quotes and techniques divided into various “ingredients for a sacred life” such as  Sleeping,  Truth,  Time and Space,  Pranayam, etc…. It is pleasant reading.
 Ram Dass makes it clear that there can be no  knower if one truly wants to  know the wisdom contained in  Be Here Now; the rational mind that separates  subject from  object is an illusion.    (Loc. 4594, 4613)   This illusion is driven by an attachment to the self as special and wholly separate from everything else.
   Deep within the cartridge of  Karateka on the Atari 7800 are a list of instructions that drive the machine. But the programmer did mix in one human-readable message with the computer code: “Mommy and Me Are One.” This bizarre statement is unique to the Atari 7800 cartridge. It does not appear in other versions. It never appears on screen. It is only visible to those who are looking at the code contained within.    “ Digital Press Easter Eggs: Karateka”,  digitpress.com, 2010.
 The phrase is almost certainly related to a paper published by Lloyd Silverman and Joel Weinberger in a 1985 edition of  American Psychologist entitled “ Mommy and I Are One: Implications For Psychotherapy”. The authors assert that the titular message can help motivate people even when stated subliminally.
 Countless studies have attempted to demonstrate the benefits of “oneness.” In the case of the mother, oneness is self-evident. We were all physically connected at one point. And deep within us exists a DNA code that has been directly passed from mother to child for millennia. This is the ancient information that sets the foundation for consciousness.
 The reality of a consciousness larger than ourselves is at the root of  Be Here Now. But rather than establish a God or a single methodology, Ram Dass continually asserts that the ego blinds us to the grand web of connections that pervade our everyday experience. The way to lift the veil is to simply practice living in the moment. Or as Suzuki Roshi is quoted, “a Zen student must learn to waste time conscientiously.”    (Loc. 4728)
  Events from this post have been added to a  timeline of significant events in the history of information.
title: 乔丹·梅克纳·卡拉特卡（Jordan Mechner Karateka）中的大师，迷幻人物和隐藏代码共同造
title_en: Oneness via gurus, psychedelics and the hidden code in Jordan Mechner's Karateka

note:
自2004年以来我就认识的乔治·梅森大学（George Mason University）著名经济学教授罗宾·汉森（Robin Hanson）自本月以来一直在奥斯汀访问经济学家，为期数周。因此，尽管我对共产主义的恐惧远远超过了罗宾，但我在德克萨斯州温和的冬天以一种与外界保持距离的方式与他会面了几次。我只花了几分钟就记住了为什么我喜欢和罗宾这么多谈话。
 看到，尽管我一直在寻找关于covid，疫苗推出，暴动，我无法专注于工作以及许多其他事情的沮丧情绪，但Robin却为他正在研究的全新数学模型而兴奋不已罗宾说，他们了解整个文明的发展。罗宾说，这一模型一口气就解释了许多宇宙之谜，并且做出了惊人的预测。我承认，我的布口罩无法保护我免受罗宾的感染热情。
 在我听的过程中，我经历了对汉森新提案的反应的经典阶段：首先，对要求我招待的内容的怪异性感到困惑，以及罗宾（Robin）未能以任何方式承认这种怪异性；然后，对于他的简明逻辑中未阐明的步骤感到困惑；接下来，我提出了许多反对意见（事实证明，罗宾已经考虑了每个反对意见）；最后，我一直都必须这样看的感觉，因为那不是很明显吗？
 Robin在一系列克服偏见的帖子中一直在解释他的模型，并且显然很快就会有关于该模型的论文。在这篇文章中，我想就Robin教给我的内容发表自己的看法。责备我捣乱的一切，都是我一个人。
 为了追逐追赶，罗宾试图解释著名的费米悖论：为什么经过60多年的观察，尽管塔比（Tabby）的恒星和“乌玛穆阿（Uumuamua）”等球星周期性地兴奋着，但我们没有看到一个无可争辩的迹象。外星文明？为什么这一切都不算什么，即使可观测的宇宙是巨大的，即使（我们现在知道的）Goldilocks区的有机分子和行星无处不在，即使外星人已经有数十亿年的时间在技术上抢先了我们，在银河系中扩展到容易被看见的地步？
 解决这个谜团的传统方法包括：也许外星人像我们很快就会在核战争或环境灾难中迅速消灭自己；也许不希望发现外星人（无论是出于自卫还是出于宇宙的总理指令）；也许他们把所有的时间都花在玩视频游戏上。至关重要的是，尽管如此，所有这样的创始人都反对这样的认识，即认识到，给定一百万种外来文明，每个与袋鼠相比可能与其他文明都不同，它们都来自乌贼，它只需要一个，跨越十亿光年，就可以改变一切。就喜欢它，我们已经注意到了。
 罗宾对这个难题的答案既简单又可怕。他说，这样的文明很可能存在，但是如果是这样的话，到我们注意到一个文明的时候，已经为时已晚。我认为，罗宾建议，如果您给一个技术文明一千万年左右的时间（即，在宇宙学时标上眨眼），那么要么 
如果认真考虑广泛传播，那么它“最大化”了这样做的技术，接近了物理定律设定的极限。
 在第一种或第二种情况下，除非我们碰巧接近文明，否则我们当然很难发现它。但是情况3呢？罗宾说，在那里，“文明”应该像一个球形，以接近光速的速度从外部看，改变一切路径。
 现在考虑一下：我们何时可以在地球上用望远镜探测到这样的球体？只有当球体的薄外壳到达地球时，才可能携带来自外星人早期历史的无线电信号，然后才开始迅速扩张。但是，到那时，不断扩大的领域本身将几乎临到我们！
 一旦进入领域，我们会发生什么？谁知道？不断扩大的文明可能会使我们丧命，可能将我们保存为动物园的动物，可能使我们融入其蜂巢的头脑中，它可能会做我们无法想象的其他事情，但是无论如何，检测到文明可能不再是有关的关注！
 （当然，人们还可能想知道，当这两个领域发生冲突时，会发生什么：他们在进行斗争吗？是否达成共识？他们会合并吗？无论答案如何，罗宾的论点都没有关系。）
 在所描述的视图上，只有一个很小的宇宙窗口可以期待SETI程序成功：即，当这些膨胀气泡中的第一个的薄表面刚刚撞击我们时，以及尚未通过我们时， 。因此，鉴于我们的“选择偏见”（即我们尚未被其中一个泡沫吞没），如果我们现在不巧在微小的检测窗口中找到自己，这也就不足为奇了！
 事实证明，这个基本建议并非罗宾所独创。确实，一位克服偏见的读者叫Daniel X. Varga向Robin指出，他（丹尼尔）在2008年就在这里（在Shtetl优化的评论线程中）分享了同样的想法！那时我一定已经读过丹尼尔·瓦尔加（Daniel Varga）的评论，但是（令人尴尬的是）它给我留下的印象不足以使我想起它。我可能和阅读这篇文章时的想法相同： 
“当然，无论如何。这是一个有趣的猜测，可能会引起一个有趣的科幻故事。遗憾的是，就像几乎所有有关外星人的故事一样，没有充分的理由比其他一百个故事都更喜欢这个故事，因为丰富的想象力可以轻松旋转。谁知道呢？”
 这就是罗宾声称要进一步发展的地方。罗宾会说，他通过建立数学模型并使模型的参数与宇宙历史的已知事实相适应，从而使它们更进一步。如果您想了解他的模型的详细信息，请阅读克服偏见或Robin即将发表的论文。就个人而言，我承认我对这些细节的兴趣不如对定性点的兴趣，定性要点（除非我没有记错）很容易用言语解释。
 关键的认识是：当我们考虑费米悖论时，我们不仅了解事实，而且我们观察和观察并且我们看不到任何外星人。还有其他一些适合的相关数据点，与我们拥有的一个技术文明样本有关。
 对于初学者来说，事实是地球上的生命至少已经进化了约35亿年-在地球存在的大部分时间中-但生命只有十亿年了，直到太阳升起将海洋蒸发掉为止使地球几乎无法居住。换句话说，至少在这个星球上，我们已经接近尾声了。为什么会这样呢？
 罗宾说，这种模式非常适合这种模式，在这种模式中，迈向像我们这样的技术文明的过程中有一些难以置信的困难，不可能实现的步骤-这些步骤可能包括生命的起源，多细胞生命，意识，语言，或完成其中的某个步骤后，进化基本上只进行随机搜索，直到偶然发现下一步或时间用完为止。
 当然，考虑到我们在这里谈论这个话题，我们一定会发现自己身处一个星球，在这个星球上，拥有博客能力的生活必不可少的所有步骤都成功了。可能还有更多的行星在早期发展中陷入了困境。
 但是，这是有趣的部分：以成功完成的所有步骤为条件，我们应该发现自己已接近星球恒星使用寿命的终点，这是因为给定星球上可用的时间越长，那里的几率就越大。也就是说，环顾宇宙，您应该发现，在进化达到所有步骤的大多数行星上，这样做几乎耗费了行星的时间。同样，当我们回头看时，我们应该发现艰难的步伐大致均匀地隔开，每个艰难的步伐占用了整个可用时间的很大一部分。所有这些与我们所看到的非常匹配。 
可以，但是这会引起第二个难题。地球上的生命至少约有35亿年之久，而可观察到的宇宙则约为137亿年之久。暂时忘掉这两个时标的时常强调的巨大，只关注它们的比率，该比率仅为〜4。地球上的生命可以追溯到大爆炸的四分之一。即使在青春期，我仍记得发现这种惊人的感觉，而不是我想像的那样。如果我只能弄清楚什么的话，这似乎显然是某种线索。
 一旦您意识到，即使太阳将在十亿年内使海洋沸腾（然后死亡数十亿个），其他恒星（主要是矮星）将继续明亮地发光数十亿年，这个难题就更加复杂了。诚然，矮星在生活上似乎不像太阳般的恒星好客，但它们看起来确实有点好客，而且确实会有很多，实际上比太阳般的恒星还要多。而且它们的使用寿命会更长。
 综上所述，我们相对于太阳生命的时间位置使地球上的生命看起来像是从巨大的宇宙泊松过程中吸取的幸运。相比之下，我们相对于所有恒星寿命的位置使我们看起来像是疯狂地，疯狂地到达，而不是您在随机模型下所期望的。那有什么呢？
 罗宾认为，所有这些事实都是在他的泡沫情景下解释的。他说，如果我们要像人类那样遥遥地体验，那么我们就必须相对接近时间的起点，因为从现在起千亿年以来，宇宙很可能会被近光所主导。迅速扩大情报领域，像我们这样的新贵文明将不再有机会。就是说，即使我们的生存是由于一些幸运的事故造成的，即使那些同样的事故可能在整个宇宙中再次发生，我们也不应该再看到其他任何事故，因为如果我们确实看到它们，那也已经差不多了我们迟到了。
 罗宾（Robin）承认，他的说法留下了一个巨大的问题：即，为什么我们的经历应该完全是“纯粹的人类”，“泡沫前”的经历？如果您认为这些不断扩大的泡沫即将到来，似乎内部的情感体验比外部的情感体验要多万亿倍。因此，像我们这样的经历将是罕见且异常的-就像与Hammurabi等人在人类历史的曙光中发现自己一样，并意识到几乎所有可能发生的事情仍在未来。因此，罗宾简单地接受一个事实，即我们的经历是“类地球”或“类人”。然后，他尝试从该起点解释其他观察结果。
 请注意，在罗宾的场景中，宇宙的当前时代是非常特殊的：这是文明刚刚形成的时候，也许其中一些文明将实现技术提升，但是在一个或多个文明为人类重新创造整个创造之前它自己的目的。现在是时候，像我们这样的早期有智慧的生物仍然可以向外看，看到四千万颗没有明显目的的恒星，只是将所有核燃料浪费在一个几乎为空的宇宙中，等待某个人出现并把能量放到很好用。在这方面，我们就像是刚登陆新西兰的毛利人，或者是比尔·盖茨（Bill Gates）于1975年对微计算机软件产业进行的调查。我们非常幸运。情况很不平衡。我们面前的千载难逢的机会不可能永远持续下去。
 如果我们接受上述观点，那么我要问的主要问题就是宇宙学的作用。 1998年，天文学家发现当前的宇宙时代之所以特别，其原因与罗宾所谈论的完全不同。也就是说，现在是物质和暗能量对宇宙能量预算的贡献大致相似的时候，前者约为30％，后者约为70％。因此，数十亿年，宇宙将越来越多地被黑暗能量所控制。随着暗能量推动星系彼此之间以及与我们之间越来越远，我们的可观察区域将变得越来越稀疏，越来越多的星系退离地平线，我们可以以光速从它们接收信号。 （特别是，这意味着，如果您想从这里访问数十亿光年的银河系，则最好还是在可以的时候开始！） 
因此，这是我的问题：这是一个偶然的巧合吗？现在是时候，宇宙处于“争夺之地”，潜在地存在于相互竞争的太空文明之间，而此时正处于物质与暗能量之间？请注意，在2007年，Bousso等人。试图为宇宙常数Λ的值给出一个复杂的人类论据，该常数测量暗能量的密度，从而测量可观测宇宙的最终大小。有关我的工作的博客帖子，请参见此处（“宇宙的数组大小”）。长话短说，出于我在博文中解释的原因，事实证明，对于人类对Λ的人类解释而言，文明仅在（或主要）在当前时代蓬勃发展，而不是在未来数万亿年蓬勃发展。如果我们必须计算很远的未来文明，那么计算将倾向于比我们实际观察到的小得多的Λ值。当然，这似乎与Robin的帐户很好地吻合。
 最后，让我以罗宾的情景的一些“实际”后果作为结束，假设我们一如既往地认真对待它。最直接的后果是，SETI的前景比您在内部化所有这些之前所想的要黯淡。 （即使对它进行了分析，但我仍然希望比起我们目前的文明，至少有一个数量级的资源更多地用于SETI。Robin的假设可能是错误的！）
 但是第二个结果是，如果我们希望人类起源的情感在整个宇宙中传播，那么我们越早开始就越好！就像1975年的比尔·盖茨（Bill Gates）一样，我们应该期望很快就会有竞争对手。确实，可能已经有竞争对手“已经”（在宇宙微波背景的其余框架中“已经”意味着）–只是来自他们的光还没有到达我们手中。因此，如果我们要确定自己的宇宙命运，而不是让奇异后的外星人为我们确定命运，那么过去将我们作为一个物种团结起来就已经过去了。我们这样做可能还只有几亿年。
note_en:
This month  Robin Hanson, the famous and  controversy-prone George Mason University economics professor who I’ve known since 2004, was visiting economists here in Austin for a few weeks. So, while my fear of covid considerably exceeds Robin’s, I met with him a few times in the mild Texas winter in an outdoor, socially-distanced way. It took only a few minutes for me to remember why I enjoy talking to Robin so much.
 See, while I’d been moping around depressed about covid, the vaccine rollout, the insurrection, my inability to focus on work, and a dozen other things, Robin was bubbling with excitement about a brand-new mathematical model he was working on to understand the growth of civilizations across the universe—a model that, Robin said, explained lots of cosmic mysteries in one fell swoop and also made striking predictions. My cloth facemask was, I confess, unable to protect me from Robin’s infectious enthusiasm.
 As I listened, I went through the classic stages of reaction to a new Hansonian proposal: first, bemusement over the sheer weirdness of what I was being asked to entertain, as well as Robin’s failure to acknowledge that weirdness in any way whatsoever; then, confusion about the unstated steps in his radically-condensed logic; next, the raising by me of numerous objections (each of which, it turns out, Robin has already thought through); finally, the feeling that I  must have seen it this way all along, because isn’t it kind of obvious?
 Robin has been explaining his model in a  sequence  of   Overcoming  Bias  posts, and will apparently have a paper out about the model soon. In this post, I’d like to offer my own take on what Robin taught me. Blame for anything I mangle lies with me alone.
 To cut to the chase, Robin is trying to explain the famous  Fermi Paradox: why, after 60+ years of looking, and despite the periodic excitement around  Tabby’s star and  ‘Oumuamua and the like, have we not seen a single undisputed sign of an extraterrestrial civilization? Why all this nothing, even though the observable universe is vast, even though (as we now know) organic molecules and planets in Goldilocks zones are everywhere, and even though there have been billions of years for aliens someplace to get a technological head start on us, expanding across a galaxy to the point where they’re easily seen?
 Traditional answers to this mystery include: maybe the extraterrestrials quickly annihilate themselves in nuclear wars or environmental cataclysms, just like we soon will; maybe the extraterrestrials don’t  want to be found (whether out of self-defense or a cosmic Prime Directive); maybe they spend all their time playing video games. Crucially, though, all answers of that sort founder against the realization that, given a million alien civilizations, each perhaps more different from the others than kangaroos are from squid,  it would only take one, spreading across a billion light-years and transforming everything to its liking, for us to have noticed it.
 Robin’s answer to the puzzle is as simple as it is terrifying. Such civilizations might well exist, he says, but if so, by the time we noticed one, it would already be nearly too late. Robin proposes, plausibly I think, that if you give a technological civilization 10 million or so years—i.e., an eyeblink on cosmological timescales—then  either
 if it’s serious about spreading widely, then it “maxes out” the technology with which to do so, approaching the limits set by physical law.
 In cases 1 or 2, the civilization will of course be hard for us to detect, unless it happens to be close by. But what about case 3? There, Robin says, the “civilization” should look from the outside like a sphere expanding at nearly the speed of light, transforming everything in its path.
 Now think about it: when could we, on earth, detect such a sphere with our telescopes? Only when the sphere’s thin outer shell had reached the earth—perhaps carrying radio signals from the extraterrestrials’ early history,  before their rapid expansion started. By that point, though, the expanding sphere itself would be nearly upon us!
 What would happen to us once we were inside the sphere? Who knows? The expanding civilization might obliterate us, it might preserve us as zoo animals, it might merge us into its hive-mind, it might do something else that we can’t imagine, but in any case,  detecting the civilization would presumably no longer be the relevant concern!
 (Of course, one could also wonder what happens when two of these spheres collide: do they fight it out? do they reach some agreement? do they merge? Whatever the answer, though, it doesn’t matter for Robin’s argument.)
 On the view described, there’s only a tiny cosmic window in which a SETI program could be expected to succeed: namely, when the thin surface of the first of these expanding bubbles has just hit us, and when it hasn’t yet passed us by. So, given our “selection bias”—meaning, the fact that we  haven’t yet been swallowed up by one of these bubbles—it’s no surprise if we don’t right now happen to find ourselves in the tiny detection window!
 This basic proposal, it turns out, is not original to Robin. Indeed, an  Overcoming Bias reader named Daniel X. Varga  pointed out to Robin that he (Daniel)  shared the same idea right here—in a  Shtetl-Optimized comment thread—back in 2008! I must have read Daniel Varga’s comment then, but (embarrassingly) it didn’t make enough of an impression for me to have remembered it. I probably thought the same as  you probably thought while reading this post:
 “Sure, whatever. This is an amusing speculation that could make for a fun science-fiction story. Alas, like with virtually  every story about extraterrestrials, there’s no good reason to favor this over a hundred other stories that a fertile imagination could just as easily spin. Who the hell knows?”
 This is where Robin claims to take things further. Robin would say that he takes them further by developing a mathematical model, and fitting the parameters of the model to the known facts of cosmic history. Read  Overcoming Bias, or Robin’s forthcoming paper, if you want to know the details of his model. Personally, I confess I’m less interested in those details than I am in the qualitative points, which (unless I’m mistaken) are easy enough to explain in words.
 The key realization is this: when we contemplate the Fermi Paradox, we know more than the mere fact that we look and look and we don’t see any aliens. There are other relevant data points to fit, having to do with the one sample of a technological civilization that we  do have.
 For starters, there’s the fact that life on earth has been evolving for at least ~3.5 billion years—for most of the time the earth has existed— but life has a mere billion more years to go, until the expanding sun boils away the oceans and makes the earth barely habitable. In other words, at least on  this planet, we’re already relatively close to the end. Why should that be?
 It’s an excellent fit, Robin says, to a model wherein there are a few incredibly difficult, improbable steps along the way to a technological civilization like ours—steps that might include the origin of life, of multicellular life, of consciousness, of language, of something else—and wherein, having achieved some step, evolution basically just does a random search until it either stumbles onto the next step or else runs out of time.
 Of course, given that we’re here to talk about it, we necessarily find ourselves on a planet where all the steps necessary for blog-capable life happen to have succeeded. There might be vastly more planets where evolution got stuck on some earlier step.
 But here’s the interesting part: conditioned on all the steps having succeeded, we  should find ourselves near the end of the useful lifetime of our planet’s star—simply because the more time is available on a given planet, the better the odds there. I.e., look around the universe and you should find that, on  most of the planets where evolution achieves all the steps, it nearly runs out the planet’s clock in doing so. Also, as we look back, we should find the hard steps roughly evenly spaced out, with each one having taken a good fraction of the whole available time. All this is an excellent match for what we see.
 OK, but it leads to a second puzzle. Life on earth is at least ~3.5 billion years old, while the observable universe is ~13.7 billion years old. Forget for a moment about the oft-stressed enormity of these two timescales and concentrate on their  ratio, which is merely ~4.    Life on earth stretches a full quarter of the way back in time to the Big Bang. Even as an adolescent, I remember finding that striking, and not at all what I would’ve guessed  a priori. It seemed like obviously a clue to  something, if I could only figure out what.
 The puzzle is compounded once you realize that, even though the sun will boil the oceans in a billion years (and then die in a few billion more), other stars, primarily dwarf stars, will continue shining brightly for  trillions more years. Granted, the dwarf stars don’t seem quite as hospitable to life as sun-like stars, but they do seem  somewhat hospitable, and there will be  lots of them—indeed, more than of sun-like stars. And they’ll last orders of magnitude longer.
 To sum up, our temporal position relative to the lifetime of the sun makes it look as though life on earth was just a lucky draw from a gigantic cosmic  Poisson process. By contrast, our position relative to the lifetime of  all the stars makes it look as though we arrived crazily, freakishly early—not at all what you’d expect under a random model. So what gives?
 Robin contends that  all of these facts are explained under his bubble scenario. If we’re to have an experience remotely like the human one, he says, then we  have to be relatively close to the beginning of time—since hundreds of billions of years from now, the universe will likely be dominated by near-light-speed expanding spheres of intelligence, and a little upstart civilization like ours would no longer stand a chance. I.e., even though our existence is down to some lucky accidents, and even though those same accidents probably recur throughout the cosmos, we shouldn’t yet  see any of the other accidents, since if we did see them, it would already be nearly too late for us.
 Robin admits that his account leaves a huge question open: namely, why should our experience have been a “merely human,” “pre-bubble” experience at all? If you buy that these expanding bubbles are coming, it seems likely that there will be trillions of times more sentient experiences inside them than outside. So experiences like ours would be rare and anomalous—like finding yourself at the dawn of human history, with Hammurabi et al., and realizing that almost everything that will ever happen is still to the future. So Robin simply accepts as a brute fact that our experience is “earth-like” or “human-like”; he then tries to explain the other observations from that starting point.
 Notice that, in Robin’s scenario, the present epoch of the universe is extremely special: it’s when civilizations are just forming, when perhaps a few of them will achieve technological liftoff, but  before one or more of the civilizations has remade the whole of creation for its own purposes. Now is the time when the early intelligent beings like us can still look out and see quadrillions of stars shining to no apparent purpose, just  wasting all that nuclear fuel in a near-empty cosmos, waiting for someone to come along and put the energy to good use. In that respect, we’re sort of like the Maoris having just landed in New Zealand, or Bill Gates surveying the microcomputer software industry in 1975. We’re ridiculously lucky. The situation is way out of equilibrium. The golden opportunity in front of us can’t possibly last forever.
 If we accept the above, then a major question I had was the role of cosmology. In 1998, astronomers discovered that the present cosmological epoch is special for a completely  different reason than the one Robin talks about. Namely, right now is when matter and  dark energy contribute roughly similarly to the universe’s energy budget, with ~30% the former and ~70% the latter. Billions of years hence, the universe will become more and more dominated by dark energy. Our observable region will get sparser and sparser, as the dark energy pushes the galaxies further and further away from each other and from us, with more and more galaxies receding past the horizon where we could receive signals from them at the speed of light. (Which means, in particular, that if you want to visit a galaxy a few billion light-years from here, you’d better start out while you still can!)
 So here’s my question: is it just a coincidence that the time—right now—when the universe is “there for the taking,” potentially poised between competing spacefaring civilizations, is  also the time when it’s poised between matter and dark energy? Note that, in 2007, Bousso et al. tried to give a  sophisticated anthropic argument for the value of the cosmological constant Λ, which measures the density of dark energy, and hence the eventual size of the observable universe.  See here for my blog post on what they did (“The array size of the universe”). Long story short, for reasons that I explain in the post, it turns out to be essential to their anthropic explanation for Λ that civilizations flourish  only (or mainly) in the present epoch, rather than trillions of years in the future. If we had to count civilizations that far into the future, then the calculations would favor values of Λ much smaller than what we actually observe. This, of course, seems to dovetail nicely with Robin’s account.
 Let me end with some “practical” consequences of Robin’s scenario, supposing as usual that we take it seriously. The most immediate consequence is that the prospects for  SETI are dimmer than you might’ve thought before you’d internalized all this. (Even  after having interalized it, I’d still like at least an order of magnitude more resources devoted to SETI than what our civilization currently spares. Robin’s assumptions might be wrong!)
 But a second consequence is that, if we want human-originated sentience to spread across the universe, then the sooner we get started the better! Just like Bill Gates in 1975, we should expect that there will soon be competitors out there. Indeed, there are likely competitors out there “already” (where “already” means, let’s say, in the rest frame of the cosmic microwave background)—it’s just that the light from them hasn’t yet reached us. So if we want to determine our own cosmic destiny, rather than having post-singularity extraterrestrials determine it for us, then it’s way past time to get our act together as a species. We might have only a few hundred million more years to do so.
title: 一旦看到它们，为时已晚
title_en: Once we can see them, it’s too late

note:
跳转至导航跳转至搜索freenode，以前称为Open Projects Network，它是一个IRC网络，用于讨论对等定向项目。 [1]可从主机名chat.freenode.net访问其服务器，该主机名通过轮流使用实际服务器来负载均衡连接。 [2] [3]另外，可在webchat.freenode.net上使用Web客户端。在2010年，它成为最大的以自由软件和开源软件为重点的IRC网络。 [4]到2013年为止，它是最大的IRC网络，[5]不管重点是什么，涵盖了超过90,000个用户和40,000个频道。
   freenode是集中管理的。职员或职员（称为IRC操作员）在所有服务器上具有相同的访问权限。可以使用/ stats p命令查看在职人员列表。通常只适用于一台服务器的某些操作（如K线）会在整个网络中传播。服务器被捐赠。而不是链接到网络。 [6]
 该网络侧重于支持同行指导和开源项目。 [7]主要主题频道以单个＃开头，希望使用该频道的组可以向freenode正式注册。 ＆＃34;关于＆＃34;频道可能与同行指导或开源项目无关，它们以两个##开头，并且以先到先得的方式提供。 [8]
  freenode当前运行Atheme IRC服务和IRCd-Seven，这是Charybdis IRCd（其自身基于Ratbox）上一组特定于freenode的补丁程序。在1999年，freenode运行了一个名为Dancer的IRCd（基于IRC-Hybrid），然后在2005年切换到Hyperion。然后，Hyperion在2010年1月30日被IRCd-Seven [9]取代。[10] [11]
    freenode最初是在另一个IRC网络EFnet上的4人Linux支持渠道#LinPeople开始的。到1995年，在迁移到Undernet之后再迁移到DALnet之后，它从一个频道转变为自己的网络irc.linpeople.org。 1998年初，它改为拥有大约200个用户和20个以下频道的Open Projects Net（OPN）。 [12] OPN很快成长为免费软件社区的最大网络，并且是世界第20大网络。 2002年，名称更名为freenode，并建立了对等项目中心（PDPC）。 [13] [14] PDPC是2002年[7]到2010年左右的注册IRS 501（c）（3）慈善机构，[15]在此期间，它在2007年获得了Linux基金会等组织的支持。[16]
 2006年6月24日，昵称为＆＃34; ratbert＆＃34;的用户获得了freenode管理员Rob Levin（lilo）的管理特权，并控制了网络。结果大概有25个用户密码被盗。 [17]该用户继续对许多freenode工作人员进行K线工作，随后大多数freenode服务器都停机了几个小时。 [18]
 2010年1月30日左右，在freenode上发现了新的攻击，这是野外从未见过的。互联网巨魔组织美国盖伊·黑格协会创建了一段JavaScript，该JavaScript导致基于Mozilla的浏览器（如Firefox和SeaMonkey）的用户静默连接到freenode并对其进行泛洪。该漏洞利用Firefox的功能将Web表单提交到80以外的端口（默认HTTP端口）。尽管Firefox开发人员在一段时间前已阻止了大多数端口，但[19]端口6667（用于IRC的端口）并未被阻止。 [20] [21] 
2014年2月2日，freenode遭受了DDoS攻击（在Twitter上由@freenodestaff确认），导致部分中断。 [22]
 2014年2月22日，freenode遭受了另一次DDoS攻击[23]，该攻击导致部分中断，随后又有几个僵尸网络试图攻击#freenode，但被重定向到＃freenode-unreg。攻击后，几台服务器仍被其提供者零路由，并且在很短的时间内，只有一台轮换的服务器正在接受连接。 [24]
 2014年9月13日，发生了DDoS攻击，导致网络分裂了几个小时，随后在#freenode通道中针对freenode的服务进行了多个僵尸网络攻击。 freenode的基础架构团队注意到其中一台IRC服务器上存在漏洞。到目前为止，该团队仅设法确定服务器被未知第三方破坏的迹象。 freenode建议出于安全原因，所有用户都应更改其NickServ密码，并已使受感染的服务器暂时脱机，直到该漏洞得到修复。 [25] [26] 2014年10月14日发布了对攻击中使用的rootkit的深入技术分析。[27]
  2017年4月14日，宣布将freenode出售给London Trust Media，作为Private Internet Access开展业务。 [29]
 2017年8月17日，freenode遭受了相当广泛的spambot攻击...包含儿童色情图片。在抵抗攻击的过程中，运营商意外设置了一条K-Line来禁止大多数网络用户。 [30]第二年，spambot攻击继续进行，涉及更多的网络，被称为＆＃34; Freenodegate＆＃34;，[31] [32]看到针对freenode管理员的站点创建受到指控恋童癖和他们的反应是通过编辑服务器配置（IRC用户模式）来阻止这些消息的传播并向用户保证。 [33]
  对等项目中心（PDPC）是运行[34] freenode IRC网络的组织，[35]许多著名的开源项目都托管着其官方IRC频道。 PDPC在英格兰和威尔士注册成立。
 PDPC的创建是为了运行freenode网络并建立与对等导向的项目社区有关的各种程序。根据其章程，PDPC的存在是“帮助以同行为导向的项目社区蓬勃发展”，主要围绕免费和开源软件项目为基础，并通过支持免费软件的开发来鼓励使用免费软件。 GNU项目[36]使用freenode网络进行通信。 
PDPC由Rob Levin创立并最初由其领导。 2006年11月，董事会进行了改组，并安装了新成员。 Seth Schoen离开了，freenode高级职员Christel Dahlskjaer成为了Schoen所在地的freenode的秘书和职员负责人。 Rob的兄弟David Levin也加入了董事会。 [37]
 2013年3月，PDPC解散。决定解散的部分原因是捐赠水平和与其维持英国慈善组织地位有关的成本。 [34]
  罗伯特·莱文（Robert Levin，1955年12月16日至2006年9月16日）[38] [39]也被称为lilo，是freenode IRC网络的创始人和PDPC慈善机构的执行董事，该基金会为freenode筹集资金。 [13]列文（Levin）自1968年以来一直是计算机程序员，从1978年到去世一直担任管理员和应用程序程序员。 [需要引用]
 2006年9月12日，莱文在得克萨斯州休斯顿市夜间骑自行车时被​​撞车撞死。发生碰撞后，据报道莱文住院了几天。他于9月16日死亡。 [38]
  从1994年开始，Levin一直鼓励在自由软件和开源项目中使用IRC。莱文是OpenProjects网络（OPN）的创始人之一，该网络迅速成长为自由软件社区使用的最大IRC网络。 OpenProjects域后来被出售，但没有出售。 [40]
 2003年，《英国名册》（The Register，UK）报道说，莱文管理原本用于OPN的资金管理不善，并引用莱文的话说，他们去付账单，付房租，买食物和必需品。 [40]

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  freenode, formerly known as  Open Projects Network, is an  IRC  network used to discuss peer-directed projects.  [1] Their servers are accessible from the host names chat.freenode.net, which  load balances connections by using the actual servers in rotation.  [2]  [3] In addition, a web client is available at webchat.freenode.net. In 2010, it became the largest  free and open source software-focused IRC network;  [4] as of 2013, it was the largest IRC network,  [5] regardless of focus, encompassing more than 90,000 users and 40,000 channels.
   freenode is centrally managed. Staffers or staff (as IRC operators are called) have the same access across all servers. A list of active staff can be viewed using the  /stats p command. Some operations that would normally only apply to one server (like K-lines) are propagated across the whole network. Servers are "donated" to the network, rather than "linked".  [6]
 The network focuses on supporting peer-directed and open-source projects.  [7] Primary on-topic channels begin with a single #, and groups wanting to use such a channel may officially register with freenode. "About" channels, which may not be about a peer-directed or open-source project, begin with two ##, and are available on a first-come, first-served basis.  [8]
  freenode currently runs Atheme IRC Services and IRCd-Seven, a set of freenode-specific patches on the Charybdis IRCd (itself based on Ratbox). In 1999, freenode ran an IRCd called Dancer (based on IRC-Hybrid), then switched to Hyperion in 2005. Hyperion was then replaced with IRCd-Seven  [9] on 30 January 2010.  [10]  [11]
    freenode began as a 4-person  Linux support channel called #LinPeople on  EFnet, another IRC network. By 1995, after moving to  Undernet, and then to  DALnet, it moved from being just a channel to its own network, irc.linpeople.org. In early 1998, it changed to Open Projects Net (OPN) with about 200 users and under 20 channels.  [12] OPN soon grew to become the largest network for the  free software community, and 20th largest in the world. In 2002, the name changed to freenode and the Peer-Directed Projects Center (PDPC) was founded.  [13]  [14] PDPC was a registered  IRS  501(c)(3) charity from 2002  [7] until approximately 2010,  [15] during which it received support from such organizations as the  Linux Fund in 2007.  [16]
 On 24 June 2006, a user with the nickname "ratbert" gained administrative privileges of freenode  administrator Rob Levin ( lilo) and took control of the network. It is likely that approximately 25 user passwords were stolen as a result.  [17] This user proceeded to  K-line many freenode staff members, and most freenode servers subsequently went down for several hours.  [18]
 Around 30 January 2010, a new attack was discovered on freenode that had never been seen before in the wild.  Internet troll organization  Gay Nigger Association of America created a piece of  JavaScript that caused users of  Mozilla-based browsers such as  Firefox and  SeaMonkey to silently connect to freenode and  flood it. This exploit used an ability of Firefox to  submit web forms to a  port other than 80 (the default  HTTP port). Whilst Firefox developers had blocked most ports some time ago,  [19] port 6667, the port for IRC, was not blocked.  [20]  [21]
 On 2 February 2014, freenode suffered a  DDoS attack (confirmed by @freenodestaff on Twitter) which caused a partial outage.  [22]
 On 22 February 2014, freenode suffered another DDoS attack  [23] which caused partial outage, followed by several botnets which attempted to attack #freenode but were redirected to #freenode-unreg. After the attacks, several servers remained nullrouted by their providers and, for a short time period, only a single server in rotation was accepting connections.  [24]
 On 13 September 2014, a DDoS attack occurred which caused the network to split for several hours, followed by several botnet attacks in the #freenode channel and against freenode's services. freenode's infrastructure team noticed a vulnerability on one of their IRC servers. So far, the team only managed to identify indication of the server being compromised by an unknown third party. freenode recommended that all users change their NickServ password for safety reasons, and has temporarily taken the compromised server offline until the vulnerability is fixed.  [25]  [26] A deep technical analysis of the rootkit used in the attack was released on 14 October 2014.  [27]
  On 14 April 2017, it was announced that freenode had been sold to London Trust Media doing business as  Private Internet Access.  [29]
 On 17 August 2017, freenode suffered from a "fairly extensive spambot attack ... containing child pornography images." In the midst of combating the attack, the operators accidentally set a  K-Line banning most users of the network.  [30]The spambot attack continued in the following year, involved even more networks and was called "Freenodegate",  [31]  [32] seeing the creation of sites against the administrators of freenode with accusations to favor pedophilia and their reaction through editing the server configuration (IRC usermodes), to stem the spread of those messages and reassure users.  [33]
  The Peer-Directed Projects Center ( PDPC) is known as the organization which ran  [34] the freenode  IRC network,  [35] where many prominent open source projects host their official IRC channels. The PDPC was incorporated in  England and Wales.
 PDPC was created to run the freenode network and to establish a variety of programs relating to peer-directed project communities. According to its charter, the PDPC exists "to help peer-directed project communities flourish", mostly based around  free and open source software projects, and encouraging the use of free software through supporting its development. The GNU Project  [36] uses the freenode network for communication.
 The PDPC was founded and initially directed by Rob Levin. In November 2006, the board went through a reshuffle and new members were installed. Seth Schoen left and Christel Dahlskjaer, senior freenode staffer, became the secretary and head of staff on freenode in Schoen's place. Also joining the board was David Levin, Rob's brother.  [37]
 In March 2013, the PDPC was dissolved. The decision to dissolve was made in part due to the donation levels and costs associated with maintaining its status as a charitable organization in the UK.  [34]
  Robert Levin (16 December 1955 – 16 September 2006),  [38]  [39] also known as   lilo, was the founder of the freenode  IRC network and Executive Director of the PDPC charity that helped fund freenode.  [13] A computer programmer since 1968, Levin worked as an administrator and an applications programmer from 1978 until his death. [   citation needed]
 On 12 September 2006, Levin was struck by a car while riding a bicycle at night in  Houston, Texas, in a  hit-and-run collision. After the collision, it was reported that Levin was hospitalized for several days. He died on 16 September.  [38]
  From 1994 onwards, Levin worked to encourage the use of IRC for  Free Software and  Open Source projects. Levin was one of the founders of the OpenProjects Network (OPN), which quickly grew to become the largest IRC network used by the free software community. The OpenProjects domains were later put up for sale, but did not sell.  [40]
 In 2003,   The Register (UK) reported that Levin  mismanaged funds intended for the OPN, quoting him as writing that they went "to paying bills, to paying the rent, to buying food and necessities."  [40]
